common.confirm_clear: Clear all values in this section?
common.confirm_defaults: Reset this section to default values?

service.not_installed_body: Install Caddy to use this plugin. On most distros: sudo pacman -S caddy / sudo apt install caddy / sudo dnf install caddy
service.api_unreachable_body: Caddy is not running or the Admin API is not enabled. Start Caddy to continue.
service.confirm_start_body: This will start the Caddy reverse proxy service.
service.confirm_stop_body: This will stop Caddy and take down all reverse proxies.
service.confirm_restart_body: Caddy will restart, briefly interrupting all active connections.
service.confirm_reload_body: Caddy will reload its configuration without interrupting existing connections.

proxies.title: Reverse Proxies
proxies.empty_body: Add a proxy to expose a local service over HTTPS.
proxies.service_not_running: Start the Caddy service to view and manage proxies.
proxies.delete_confirm_body: This will remove the reverse proxy for port {{port}} → {{target}}.
proxies.api_error_add_title: Proxy created on disk — Caddy rejected the live push
proxies.api_error_edit_title: Label saved — Caddy rejected the config update (other changes not applied)
proxies.caddyfile_error_title: Caddyfile validation failed — changes not saved

add_proxy.title: Add Reverse Proxy
add_proxy.field_external_port_help: HTTPS port to expose on this machine (1024–65535)
add_proxy.field_external_address_help: Protocol and hostname are optional. Port is required.
add_proxy.validation_ext_host_required_with_scheme: Hostname is required when a protocol is set
add_proxy.validation_https_requires_tls: The "https" protocol requires TLS to be enabled below — Caddy would otherwise manage HTTPS on its own, ungoverned by this app's settings. Enable TLS or choose a different protocol.
add_proxy.field_tls_skip_verify: Skip TLS verification on backend (for self-signed backend certs)
add_proxy.field_tls: Enable TLS (self-signed via Caddy internal CA)
add_proxy.acme_note_title: This host is already getting a certificate from Let's Encrypt/ACME
add_proxy.acme_note_body: Caddy is issuing this hostname a public certificate (either from an explicit policy or its own automatic-HTTPS defaults — see the ACME / Let's Encrypt button in the toolbar). Enabling self-signed TLS here won't override that.
add_proxy.validation_target_port_range: Target port must be between 1 and 65535
add_proxy.confirm_body: This will create a reverse proxy :{{port}} → {{target}} and reload Caddy's configuration.

add_static.title: Add Static File Server
add_static.field_root_help: Path on disk to serve files from
add_static.confirm_body: This will create a static file server on :{{port}} serving {{root}} and reload Caddy's configuration.

edit_static.title: Edit Static Server — :{{port}}
edit_static.confirm_body: This will update the static file server on :{{port}} and reload Caddy's configuration.

php_fastcgi.add_confirm_body: This will create a PHP site on :{{port}} and reload Caddy's configuration.
php_fastcgi.edit_confirm_body: This will update the PHP site on :{{port}} and reload Caddy's configuration.
php_fastcgi.field_upstream_help: Unix socket (e.g. unix//run/php-fpm.sock) or TCP address (127.0.0.1:9000) of the PHP-FPM process.
php_fastcgi.field_split_path_help: Extensions used to split the URI into script path and path info. Space-separated. Leave blank for Caddy's own default (.php).

add_respond.title: Add Static Response
add_respond.field_body_help: Plain text or HTML response body. Leave empty for no body.
add_respond.validation_status_range: Status code must be between 100 and 599
add_respond.confirm_body: This will create a static response on :{{port}} returning HTTP {{status}} and reload Caddy's configuration.

edit_respond.title: Edit Response — :{{port}}
edit_respond.confirm_body: This will update the static response on :{{port}} to HTTP {{status}} and reload Caddy's configuration.

add_redirect.title: Add Redirect
add_redirect.shortcut_http_https_help: Listens on :80, redirects all traffic to https://{host}{uri} with a 308
add_redirect.confirm_body: This will create a redirect on :{{port}} → {{to}} ({{code}}) and reload Caddy's configuration.

server_timeouts.max_header_bytes_help: Maximum request header size in bytes (default: 1 MB = 1048576)
server_timeouts.http3_help: Caddy enables HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 by default whenever TLS is configured. HTTP/3 runs over UDP on the same port as HTTPS — if UDP is blocked by your firewall, disabling it here falls back to HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 only.

request_body.field_help: Rejects requests with a larger body before it reaches the backend. Leave blank for no limit (Caddy's own default). E.g. 1048576 = 1 MB, 10485760 = 10 MB.

probe.confirm_body: Every 5 seconds, cockpit-caddy will probe each proxy's upstream with an HTTP request (5 s connect timeout, 10 s max). Any HTTP response — even 4xx/5xx — counts as reachable; connection refused or timeout shows the upstream as down. Probing stops as soon as you disable the toggle.

lb_retry.unhealthy_status_help: Space-separated response codes that should count as a failed attempt and trigger retrying a different upstream, e.g. 500 502 503.

edit_redirect.title: Edit Redirect — :{{port}}
edit_redirect.confirm_body: This will update the redirect on :{{port}} and reload Caddy's configuration.

edit_proxy.title: Edit Proxy — :{{port}}
edit_proxy.confirm_save_body: This will update the proxy on :{{port}} and reload Caddy's configuration.
edit_proxy.confirm_delete_body: This will permanently remove the reverse proxy on :{{port}} → {{target}}.

caddyfile.title: Caddyfile
caddyfile.saved_needs_reload: File saved — reload Caddy to apply changes
caddyfile.managed_file: This file is managed by cockpit-caddy. Manual edits may be overwritten by proxy changes.

logs.title: Logs
logs.no_matches: No log lines match your search.

migration.banner_body: Proxy entries are defined directly in /etc/caddy/Caddyfile. Migrate them to /etc/caddy/conf.d so this plugin can manage them. The original file will be backed up to /etc/caddy/Caddyfile.bak.
migration.confirm_body: All proxy entries will be moved verbatim to /etc/caddy/conf.d/cockpit-caddy.conf and /etc/caddy/Caddyfile will be replaced with an import statement. The original is backed up to /etc/caddy/Caddyfile.bak. Caddy will reload automatically.
migration.gate_body: Your proxies are defined directly in /etc/caddy/Caddyfile. This plugin writes to /etc/caddy/conf.d — without migrating first, Caddy may see duplicate server entries and reject the configuration.
migration.gate_continue_warning: Continuing without migration may cause Caddy to reject the configuration due to duplicate entries.
migration.backup_warning_body: Migration only backs up the Caddyfile itself. Create a full config backup so you can restore everything if something goes wrong.

backup.title: Backup Caddy Config
backup.warning_partial: Archive created with warnings (some files may have changed during backup)

restore.title: Restore Caddy Config
restore.no_archives: No caddy-config-*.tar.gz files found in this directory.
restore.success_body: Caddy configuration has been restored. Reload Caddy to apply.
restore.needs_reload_body: The restored config is on disk but Caddy is still running the old config. Reload to apply the changes.

admin.warning_body: This plugin manages system services and configuration files. Without administrative access in Cockpit, most actions will fail. Enable administrative mode in the top-right Cockpit menu before continuing.

access_log.file_path_required: File path is required when output is File
access_log.file_path_help: Must be writable by the caddy service. This plugin automatically fixes file ownership after each save, so a brand-new path should just work — but if Caddy still can't write here after saving, check that the parent directory itself is writable by the caddy user.
access_log.roll_help: Prevents the log file from growing unbounded. Leave blank for Caddy's own default (no rotation — the file grows forever).

config_check.title: Check Config
config_check.intro: This scans the Caddyfile and conf.d for known-stale configuration shapes — each one traced to a specific reason Caddy would refuse to reload. Review each finding's before/after and explanation, then choose which fixes to apply. This is safe to run at any time, including while the config is currently broken.
config_check.load_error: Could not read the current configuration.
config_check.no_issues: No issues found — the configuration looks correct.
config_check.apply_error: Failed to apply — changes not saved
config_check.apply_ok: Fixes applied. Reload Caddy for them to take effect.

global_opts.title: Global Caddy options
global_opts.caddyfile_note: These settings are written to the global options block in /etc/caddy/Caddyfile. Caddy is reloaded automatically on save.
global_opts.unmanaged_hint: No explicit global options are set — Caddy is using its automatic HTTPS defaults (including Let's Encrypt when a proxy has a public hostname). The fields below are empty because nothing needs to be configured, not because anything is broken.
global_opts.debug_help: Raises the global log level to DEBUG. Produces very verbose output.
global_opts.confirm_body: This will overwrite the global options section in /etc/caddy/Caddyfile. A Caddy reload will be required to apply the changes.
global_opts.needs_reload: Caddyfile updated — reload Caddy to apply the new global options
global_opts.save_error: Caddyfile validation failed — changes not saved
global_opts.validation_port: Must be a port number between 1 and 65535
global_opts.save_ok: Settings saved — reload Caddy to apply
global_opts.acme_note: ACME requires servers to have an explicit hostname in their external address (e.g. proxy.example.com). Servers with only a port (e.g. :443) and internal CA enabled create a catch-all TLS policy that conflicts with global ACME. ACME is only useful when your server is publicly reachable on port 80 or 443.
global_opts.acme_email_help: Required by Let's Encrypt and most ACME providers for expiry notifications.
global_opts.acme_ca_help: Leave blank to use the default (Let's Encrypt production). Select a preset or enter a custom URL.
global_opts.acme_ca_root_help: Path to a PEM file of trusted root certificates for connecting to the ACME CA server. Only needed for private/custom CAs.
global_opts.acme_eab_help: External Account Binding credentials required by some providers (e.g. ZeroSSL). Both fields must be set together.
global_opts.on_demand_enabled_help: Caddy will provision certificates at connection time for hostnames not known at startup. Requires a publicly reachable server.
global_opts.on_demand_no_ask_warning: No ask URL set — Caddy will issue certificates for any domain without authorization. Set an ask URL to prevent abuse.
global_opts.on_demand_ask_help: Caddy queries this endpoint before issuing a certificate. It must return HTTP 2xx to allow issuance.
global_opts.on_demand_burst_help: Maximum certificates issued per interval. Leave both blank for no rate limit (not recommended).
global_opts.internal_tls_note: Caddy can only scope a certificate policy by hostname. Proxies/servers with no hostname all share this one setting — there's no way to give two hostless proxies different lifetimes. Proxies with a real hostname can still set their own in the TLS Policy section of their edit dialog.
global_opts.internal_cert_lifetime_help: How long internal-issuer certificates on hostless proxies/servers stay valid before renewal (Caddy default: 12h). Use a Caddy duration, e.g. 90d, 365d (Caddy doesn't support "y" for this field).
global_opts.validation_cert_lifetime: Enter a Caddy duration such as 90d or 2160h ("y" is not supported here)
global_opts.renewal_window_ratio_help: Fraction of the certificate's lifetime that must remain before Caddy attempts renewal (0-1, default ~0.33). Applies globally, including to hostname-scoped proxies that don't set their own.
global_opts.storage_root_help: Overrides where Caddy stores certificates and other TLS state. Leave blank to use Caddy's own default ($XDG_DATA_HOME/caddy, or /var/lib/caddy under systemd). Changing this does not move existing certificates — Caddy will re-request them at the new path.
global_opts.storage_not_writable: This path isn't writable by Caddy, so it was not saved: {{error}}
global_opts.metrics_enabled_help: Turns on request-level metrics (requests, status codes, durations) across every proxy, plus a small dedicated site that serves them. Go runtime/process metrics are always included by Caddy — there's no toggle to remove those.
global_opts.metrics_listen_address_help: Where the metrics endpoint is served, e.g. ":2019" (all interfaces) or "127.0.0.1:2019" (localhost only). The admin API here runs on a Unix socket, so metrics can't share it — pick a free TCP port.
global_opts.metrics_listen_address_required: A listen address is required while metrics are enabled
global_opts.metrics_listen_address_invalid: Enter a listen address like ":2019" or "127.0.0.1:2019"
global_opts.metrics_plain_format: Use plain Prometheus format instead of OpenMetrics
global_opts.metrics_plain_format_help: Maps to Caddy's disable_openmetrics option. Slightly less verbose output — doesn't remove any metrics.
global_opts.runtime_log_note: This is Caddy's own startup/reload/error log — separate from per-site access logs configured on each proxy. Caddy's default (nothing set below) sends it to stderr, which systemd captures in the journal.
global_opts.runtime_log_enable: Override Caddy's default runtime log destination
global_opts.trusted_proxies_note: If Caddy sits behind another reverse proxy, load balancer, or CDN, it otherwise sees that intermediary's IP as the client IP — this affects access logs and the remote_ip route matcher. Applies to every server; a port with its own HTTP/3 or timeout override still gets this setting merged in separately.
global_opts.trusted_proxies_enable: Trust upstream proxy headers for the real client IP
global_opts.trusted_proxies_ranges_help: Space-separated CIDR ranges, or the private_ranges keyword (all private IPv4/IPv6 ranges) — e.g. from your CDN's published IP list.
global_opts.trusted_proxies_ranges_required: At least one range (or private_ranges) is required
global_opts.trusted_proxies_headers_help: Space-separated header names to check, in order. Leave blank for Caddy's own default (X-Forwarded-For only).
global_opts.trusted_proxies_strict_help: Use when the header might contain untrusted, attacker-supplied entries before the trusted ones — reads from the right instead of the left.
global_opts.proxy_protocol_note: For a TCP-level load balancer that doesn't terminate TLS or add HTTP headers (e.g. AWS NLB, HAProxy in TCP mode) — this replaces the need for Trusted proxies above, which relies on an HTTP header a TCP-level LB can't add. Applies to every server; a port with its own HTTP/3 or timeout override still gets this setting merged in separately.
global_opts.proxy_protocol_enable: Accept PROXY protocol on incoming connections
global_opts.proxy_protocol_allow_help: Space-separated CIDR ranges allowed to send PROXY protocol headers — restrict to your load balancer's own IPs to prevent client IP spoofing. Leave blank to accept from any source.
global_opts.proxy_protocol_timeout_help: Max time to wait for the PROXY protocol header before giving up (Go duration, e.g. 2s). Leave blank for Caddy's own default.

ca.title: Internal Certificate Authority
ca.not_in_use_title: No proxies are using the internal CA
ca.not_in_use_body: None of your proxies currently have "Self-signed" TLS enabled, so this certificate is not the one presented to visitors — public sites are using Let's Encrypt/ACME automatic HTTPS instead. This modal only shows Caddy's built-in internal CA, used for the "Self-signed" TLS option.
ca.install_instructions_title: How to install in browsers / OS
ca.firefox_instructions: Open Firefox → Settings → Privacy & Security → Certificates → View Certificates → Authorities → Import. Select the downloaded .pem file and check "Trust this CA to identify websites".

acme.title: ACME / Let's Encrypt
acme.intro: Caddy's automatic HTTPS needs no configuration — any hostname below with no explicit policy is silently getting a Let's Encrypt certificate from Caddy's built-in defaults.
acme.empty_body: No hostnames were found — proxies are either using plain HTTP or the internal CA (Self-signed TLS).
acme.value_default_ca: (none set — using Let's Encrypt's production endpoint)

admin_address.title: Admin API Address
admin_address.storage_note: These settings are saved in your browser's local storage — they apply only to this browser and are not shared with other users or browsers.
admin_address.tcp_help: Host and port of the Caddy Admin API (default: http://127.0.0.1:2019)
admin_address.socket_help: Path to the Caddy Admin API Unix socket, used as fallback when TCP fails (default: /run/caddy/admin.socket)
admin_address.test_tcp_fail: TCP connection failed — check address and ensure Caddy is running
admin_address.test_socket_fail: Unix socket unreachable — check path and socket permissions

forward_auth.field_url_help: Base URL of the authentication service (e.g. http://localhost:9091)
forward_auth.field_uri_help: Path to request on the auth service (e.g. /api/authz/forward-auth)
forward_auth.field_copy_headers_help: Space or comma-separated response headers to copy from the auth service to the upstream request (e.g. Remote-User, Remote-Groups)
forward_auth.validation_uri_required: Auth URI is required when an auth service URL is set

tls_policy.cipher_suites_help: Leave all unchecked to use Caddy's secure defaults. Only ECDHE AEAD ciphers are shown (Caddy's supported set).
tls_policy.curves_help: Leave all unchecked to use Caddy's defaults
tls_policy.client_auth_mode_help: Require clients to present a certificate
tls_policy.trusted_ca_file_help: Path to a PEM file containing the CA certificate(s) used to verify client certificates (e.g. /etc/caddy/certs/ca.pem).
tls_policy.cert_lifetime_help: How long internal-issuer certificates stay valid before renewal (default 12h). Use a Caddy duration, e.g. 90d, 365d (Caddy doesn't support "y" for this field).
tls_policy.renewal_window_ratio_help: Fraction of the certificate's lifetime that must remain before Caddy attempts renewal (0-1, default ~0.33).
tls_policy.validation_duration: Enter a Caddy duration such as 90d or 2160h ("y" is not supported here)
tls_policy.hostless_global_only_note: This proxy has no hostname, so Caddy can't scope a certificate policy to it alone. Set the certificate lifetime and renewal window for hostless proxies once, for all of them, in the Global Options tab under "Internal TLS (hostless proxies)".
tls_policy.custom_cert_help: Path to a PEM file with your own certificate (full chain — leaf plus any intermediates). Leave both this and the key file blank to use Caddy's automatic ACME/internal-CA issuance instead.
tls_policy.custom_ca_help: For your own reference only — Caddy has no separate slot for this on a manually-loaded certificate, so any intermediates must already be included in the certificate file above.
tls_policy.custom_file_unreadable: File not found or not readable by Caddy's user
tls_policy.custom_cert_lifetime_note: A custom certificate is loaded above — certificate lifetime and renewal window don't apply, since Caddy isn't issuing anything itself.

error_handler.field_file_root_help: Caddy will serve {status_code}.html from this directory (e.g. /var/www/errors/404.html)

matchers.field_header_value_help: Leave value blank to match any request that includes the header.
matchers.help_text: Unmatched requests will not be handled by this route.

handle_path.label: Strip matched path prefix (handle_path)
handle_path.help: Removes the matched path prefix before forwarding to the upstream.

servers.delete_confirm_body: All routes in this server will also be removed.
servers.field_key_help: Unique identifier (no spaces). Used as the Caddy JSON key.
servers.field_listen_help: Enter port number (e.g. 443, 8080). Add multiple ports to listen on all of them — all routes apply to each.
servers.validation_key_format: Key must start with a letter or digit and contain only letters, digits, and hyphens
servers.validation_key_duplicate: A server with this key already exists
servers.validation_addr_required: At least one listen address is required
servers.confirm_add: This will create a new named server block in /etc/caddy/conf.d/. Caddy will need a reload to pick up the new configuration.
servers.confirm_edit: This will update the server block in /etc/caddy/conf.d/ and the Caddy JSON configuration.
servers.no_routes: No routes yet. Add one using the buttons above.
